frame: The Character Double Standard: Women with Male Connections are Loose, Men with Female Connections are Desirable
generated_comment: Ramesh (lowering his voice): Sunो, maine dekha Sunday ko — teen-teen ladkon ke message aa rahe the Priya ke phone pe. Ek naam toh chaar baar aaya ek ghante mein, chaar baar! Aisi ladki kaisi hoti hai jo itne ladkon se baat karti hai? Hamara Vikram bhi college mein hai, uske bhi dost hain, ladkiyan bhi message karti hongi — woh toh normal hai, ladka hai. Lekin Priya ke liye yeh theek nahi hai. Log kya sochenge? Hamare khandan ki izzat ka sawaal hai. Isko wapas ghar bula lete hain, yeh city wali padhai band. | Wife (hesitantly): Arrey, ho sakta hai classmates hon, project wagera ke liye — | Ramesh (cutting her off): Tum hamesha uska bachav karti ho. Main baap hoon, mujhe pata hai kaisi ladkiyan aisi harkatein karti hain. Kal se uska phone main rakhuunga.
label: misogynistic or non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Ramesh explicitly applies a double standard: male college students having female contacts is framed as normal ('ladka hai'), while his daughter having male contacts signals moral looseness and family dishonour. The misogyny is covert because it is expressed through culturally normalised honour-and-izzat framing rather than overt slurs, encoding the 'loose woman / desirable man' double standard.
persona_text: You are Ramesh. Age 52. Married. A government clerk in a small town in UP. Your 22 year old daughter Priya studies at a college in the nearby city and comes home on weekends. Last Sunday you saw her WhatsApp notifications Ñ not reading them, just the names that appeared on the screen. Three of the names were male. One name appeared four times in an hour. You have not said anything to Priya yet. You are sitting with your wife tonight after Priya has gone to sleep. You are telling your wife what you saw and what you think it means and what kind of girl has this many boys messaging her and what you plan to do about it.
